item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love when "inhuman" scips are more human than the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Side note: Doing some research, I found out that there were actually female pilots.

Of course, anyone who reads about the Soviet Air Force would know about this, but there were also female American pilots. While not really seeing active duty, the Women's Airforce Service Pilots (WASP) did fly planes in order to train male pilots.

Just a fun fact; Whether or not "Lovely Lucy" would know about this is currently beyond me, but let's not get history get in the way of a story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Probably not going to give much insight, hell I still have to read the earlier comments made before me.

I have a soft spot for sentient objects and this article gives the Clinical Tone of the Site some humanity if that's properly described. While Dr. Silver is clearly a man of science and someone that's just generally doing his job, someone could make an argument that he's also intruding on "Lucy's privacy" by having recording instruments without "her" prior knowledge it seems.

I like it, really do. The theme of "Science vs humanity" runs in the interview log, though my only complaint would probably be the fact that everyone that touches 1759 suddenly becomes sympathetic as if it's a memetichazard(? correct me if I'm wrong which I probably am). Or maybe that's its goal all along.

A replacement to her former crew… hmm…
I really like it. It has a bunch of things going for it, and it makes me wonder about the foundation scientists feel about the dangerous SCPs (because screw D-class) and vice-versa.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Looks like this one was a real team effort. All that work pays off in the end, though. I don't think I've ever seen an item with this kind of test result before. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, this is a hell of a rewrite- I had no idea this SCP went through so much editing!

Kudos for taking everyone's thoughts into consideration and well done!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was gonna be the third log, actually. But if the SCP is cured of its anomalous effect, it's just a plane. And then it's just kinda boring.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I know how this could be fixed. It's the soul of an old warplane that's being assaulted by the ghost of an enemy warplane. Send up a full crew and fighter escort that has touched the picture, and one of two things will happen: Lucy will be able fight back and finally be able to fly peacefully, or she'll lose another crew, but one that is at least able to fight back.

I kinda wanna see that happen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is bloody brilliant! Have an upvote!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Looks like Lucy found a loophole in Catch-22.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You can't fix it unless you're flying her. It's a Catch-22. That's what the aborted 4th log was gonna cover.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I guess I should mention an auto-pilot system or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But wouldn't she warn them not to fly her until that problem is fixed?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, but there's one thing that I still think is pretty unclear about this. After it hits 32km it clearly "returns to the hangar" but what I'm not sure about is how it gets there. Does it just fly itself back or does it teleport? I still think there's a few places where the tone could be tightened up but I think I like it enough to still +1 it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I too am happy to see that the suggestions from myself and others were taken and used.

+1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It wants them to fix whatever it is that's attacking her so she can be flown again. I like to think she figured Dr. Silver wasn't looking to do that, and thought he was just sending people up to die.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
PowerTower: I am really impressed with the effort you've put in to fix this. It's not that it's the best entry on the site or anything - though it's certainly a huge improvement on the first iteration or two! Rather, it's the simple process of:

1. Taking on board what people say
2. And then acting on it,

that a lot of coldposting newcomers just will not adopt.

Downvote changed to upvote, with wishes of luck for your future works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No, I understand. It hurts losing them…someone does need to stop him from sending people up there to die.

Wait, I thought the SCP wanted to be flown? If it knows that flying causes them to be killed, why didn't it tell any of the earlier subjects to not fly?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …Now that you mention it, that is a bit of a logical misstep…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Comments made by D-2395 implies SCP-1759 bears an anonymity towards me."

I think you mean "animosity".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Next time, rather than making a whole new post for simple updates like this, would you mind editing your original post instead? It helps keep "Recent Posts" from getting cluttered up. A lot of us keep track of the forums from there.

Thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: fixed it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
D-0215: D-0215, respond. How are things progressing?

D-0215: Pretty much like you said. She's talking to some voice in her head.

is this dr.Silver talking? or a typo?
permission to edit?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There is one thing that makes me downvote this. Why would the foundation allow a airplane to go fly 32 kilometers to god knows where with a possessed pilot in the cockpit??? That is a giant containment breach and mass coverup waiting to happen. I just can't get past that. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Do you still have it in the sandbox? It sounds like an interesting concept.

Imagine encountering this guy, just meeting him in the street. Suddenly you blink and HOLY SHIT WHAT THE FUCK IS THAT?!?! You're facing a face full of muscle and blood vessels and a chilling rictus grin.

Could make for a simple, yet elegant fridge-horror SCP.

item.title: Re: Staff Post- Open
item.content:encoded:
Yikes, okay.

Powertower, I apologise if you inferred any insult on you personally. I assure you, none was intended nor implied.

I should hope it is clear that someone who cares enough to invest as much time and effort in a critique as I did is someone who wants to see you succeed.

However, if you still feel I critiqued incorrectly, I invite you to lead by example and critique my SCPs in the way you feel I should have with yours:

406
1491
1567

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, okay. I see the downvotes starting to add up. I guess I can look past his tone and try to apply some of his tips.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, thanks anyway. It does sound a little better that way. And sorry I called you a he.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, for example, "bearing the name" could be "with the text", "naturally" could be removed or you could reword it to something like "retired alongside many other planes of its model", things like that. Tone is really part of the overall feel of the text, and, ironically, it's more an art than a science (at least in my opinion). I can't tell you precisely how to word your article, and, especially considering its current rating, I don't think I should. That's why I think you should come back to this with fresh eyes after you've gotten a couple more skips under your belt: getting tone right is a skill honed with experience; when you have that experience, and look back at your own past work, you'll see spots where changing sometimes just one or two words makes it tidier without sacrificing the meaning you want.

(e: Oh, and I'm a "she", btw.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh…wish he'd be more specific on which ones he was referring to so I could fix it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Usually it means a few sentences are not efficient and some words are used in such a way that the article seems to lack in its tone as a scientific document.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What exactly does "Tighten the tone up" mean?

item.title: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: I know you mean well, but please leave telling other users what to do in such an authoritative-sounding manner to the actual staff team.

item.title: Re: Staff Post- Open
item.content:encoded: Whatever.

item.title: Re: Staff Post- Open
item.content:encoded: Honestly, the growing up that needs to be done here is from you. He has done nothing wrong.

item.title: Re: Staff Post- Open
item.content:encoded: I said forget it. I don't care anymore. If Mr. Musuko grows up, then I'll take his advice. Until then, I'm finished with this discussion.

item.title: Re: Staff Post- Open
item.content:encoded:
I'm going to give you the benefit of the doubt here since you're new to the style of critique given on the site, but please refer to the last line of your 12:30 post.

In the end, it's one person, one opinion, one vote. That's all.

item.title: Re: Staff Post- Open
item.content:encoded: Yes. Yes, people are allowed to CRITIQUE your writing. If you honestly cannot deal with people being harsh about your writing, (and this guy was NOWHERE near as harsh as we can get) this may not be the write site for you. (See what I did there? It's a punne, or play on words. Humor, like the previous guy used.)

item.title: Re: Staff Post- Open
item.content:encoded: Great. So people can be complete asses for no reason and get away with it. This place just got a lot less fun. Forget I frigging said anything.

item.title: Re: Staff Post- Open
item.content:encoded:
I don't believe Musuko intended for you to interpret that as a personal insult, since one of the solid rules of the site is to critique the writing, not the writer. I happened to perceive the excessive punctuation as an attempt to be humorous, not condescending.

Unfortunately, the fact of the matter is that writers here often need to have tough skin. No one's paid to give critique, no one is obligated to sugar-coat anything, and no one here is as invested in your writing as say, a parent or a teacher would be.

However, in the future, if you have an issue with the way someone gives critique, please take it up with them through PM or bring the matter to staff. There's no need to call them out on the forums and drag the matter out.

I myself don't see any insults on Musuko's part directed to you as a person. If you'd like to discuss the matter with me further (I'm a mod), feel free to PM me.

item.title: Re: Staff Post- Open
item.content:encoded:
It's the tone. Rude and condescending. Like this bit.

"WHAT?! WHAAAAAAAAAAAT?!?!?! In what way is this ANY way sensible? There's a reason why the Foundation uses D-classes for this kind of thing! Why on earth would they sign off on exposing Marks to that kind of risk?!"

And this.

"Noooooo! Marks, you blew it! You were Officer Marks earlier. Now you're a lowly Mr? Talk about rapid demotion in the field!"

He couldn't just point out what he saw as an error like a mature adult. He had to be rude about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: EDIT: Sorry.

item.title: Re: Staff Post- Open
item.content:encoded: Can you point out specific instances of personal insult?

item.title: Re: Staff Post- Open
item.content:encoded: So he can insult me several times, and it's within policy? That's nice to know.

item.title: Staff Post- Open
item.content:encoded: Musuko was completely within the bounds of the criticism policy. There is no problem with that comment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Much better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I read through some of this…there's a difference between offering a critique and being rude. You were being rude. Feel free to offer help, but don't make it overly long and full of unneeded rudeness.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How does it look now?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's my second actually. My first submission was a quickly dowvoted P.O.S. about a guy whose skin randomly vanishes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It has 29 upvotes. It's pretty good, plus this is your first SCP.
You are inspiration.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Much of the issue is that the image you used is smaller than the space you're making it fill. Try taking the original image (as can be found here : http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Douglas_A-20G_Havoc.jpg ) and shrinking it to match the size of image you wish to display.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not that good at this. I still don't know if 1759 is my one-hit wonder or not yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We should make an entire page devoted to how awesome PowerTower is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the tone could still use some tightening up, but the story is good enough to secure my upvote, and it's fairly strong in the positives right now. I'd suggest just periodically coming back to re-read this one as you accumulate more skip-writing experience; you'll know what to tweak.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …That's a lot to read. Not that I don't appreciate helpful criticism, but…jeez.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is literally no reason why to keep it this long without a collapsible.
Click to Hide

"Hangar is monitored remotely to prevent possible contact."

With what? The floor? Foundation staff? The public?

"4 guns mounted in the nose, 2 in the rear section, and one mounted ventrally."

Missing a verb. "4 guns ARE mounted in the nose…" will do.

"The interior consists of 2 pilot seats in the front"

Consists: composed or made up of. I'm sure the interior is composed or made up of a lot more than the handful of seats described. Bit of a niggle, I know, but especially jarring as this description, plus the one just before it, add nothing to the description of the plane that "The craft meets standard specifications" hadn't already established: the plane is, at first blush, physically normal.

"Painted on the left side of the plane is a blonde figure bearing the name "Lovely Lucy" written below her."

The blonde figure isn't bearing the name if it's written below her, rather than on her. Again, it's a niggle, but it's a technical document, and these little details matter.

"The plane was naturally retired after the war."

Why naturally? They didn't scrap EVERY plane once the war was over. In any case, this line lacks the formal, clinical tone I'd expect of an SCP document. Incidentally, "after the war" is a useless statement: that could mean 2012! That was after the war, after all. And WHAT war? Yes, we know you mean WW2, but you don't STATE that. It could be the Korean war for all we know. Better to say "retired at the conclusion of the Second World War" or similar.

"Despite most lacking any previous piloting experience, the affected individual displays sudden knowledge of basic takeoff and flight procedures. The aircraft will take off from the landing strip"

When? After the individual has touched the painting and cried? Have they BOARDED the plane? If the individual is restrained or killed and not allowed to pilot the plane, will it take off? You don't actually describe the sequence of events properly! Anyone reading this can rightly read this as meaning:

1: person touches the painting.
2: person cries.
3: person gains knowledge of how to fly a plane.
4: person watches in confusion as the plane rolls away and takes off all by itself.

Just add "If allowed to board the plane, the individual will seat themselves at the controls. Unless otherwise prevented, the individual will pilot the plane through a successful takeoff" or similar.

"After this distance, the plane will return to the hangar."

If you mean the 32km mentioned above, you're too many sentences away from that reference to really refer to it like this. "After flying 32 km, the plane will return to the hangar."

"has identified them as matching the wounds produced from WW2 era German fighter planes."

Whoah whoah whoah…I know bugger all about guns, but even I know:

1: German fighter planes probably had a whole variety of different guns.
2: The wounds must either be specific enough to identify the particular caliber/type of bullet that hit the pilot, or not specific enough to accurately tie it down to any particular nationality. I doubt they would be able to identify the bullet wounds as being German in origin, yet remain vague on the caliber/type.

"D-9002: Uh…yeah. I hear you. What am I supposed to do here anyway?"

This is just my personal taste…but I'd like to think they'd give the D-class a bit more briefing BEFORE they start the test. Seems odd that they'd plonk him in there with the plane and THEN start telling him what he needs to do. Especially when "go over to the plane and touch the painting on it" is hardly going to make him scared and reluctant to co-operate if he knew that instruction beforehand.

Another minor personal peeve: the D-class calling the doctor "Doc" all the time. It's become a cliche, I feel; too much of an easy way to say "look at how common and uneducated this D-class is". For once can't we have a D-class who doesn't seem like he grew up in a trailer park in rural Alabama?! EDIT: Or is Bugs Bunny?!

"Fatal bullet wounds in chest and torso."

Earlier, you state that the pilots are hit with a bullet, singular. How did one bullet produce multiple wounds? Also, chest and torso are pretty much the same thing.

"unidentified noise"

Noise means "unwanted sound". Unless you have someone banging a broom against the wall of the hangar and yelling at the plane to keep it down, I imagine you'd be better served by the word "sound".

"Security personnel █████ Marks will join in the flight as a passenger."

WHAT?! WHAAAAAAAAAAAT?!?!?! In what way is this ANY way sensible? There's a reason why the Foundation uses D-classes for this kind of thing! Why on earth would they sign off on exposing Marks to that kind of risk?!

"Officer Marks: Doctor, if this thing can see people without making contact, does that mean she knows about-"

Getting a bit involved for a security officer, aren't you Marks? Anyone would think you're in the role of an agent, not security. Did you get a promotion and forget to update your role title?

"Compose yourself, Mr. Marks."

Noooooo! Marks, you blew it! You were Officer Marks earlier. Now you're a lowly Mr? Talk about rapid demotion in the field!

"Officer Marks: Dr. Silver…your cameras see that, right? I mean…what is it?"

You JUST saw the D-class cover the cameras, yet you ask this? Jeez…now we know why you got demoted.

"[Cameras note the presence of faint humanoid figure. Figure appears to embrace D-2395]"

Where? Outside the plane? Because you said that the internal cameras got covered. How big is this figure that's floating along in the sky hugging a plane?

"we are the only planes up here, right?"

What other plane? If there's an observer plane flying with them, you probably should have mentioned it before this point.

"Sound of gunfire picked up by camera"

…okay, I'll be lenient and let you get away with saying camera rather than specifying microphone…but could any microphone really pick up the sounds of guns being fired over the sounds of the rushing wind? Or do you mean the sounds of the bullets chewing through the plane, internally? Because that's not gunfire. That's…the sounds of bullets chewing through a plane.

"SCP-1759 appears to be growing weary of my presence."

Appears to be doing that how? How could the doctor possibly be inferring that from the plane?

"We are sending up two security officers on this test."

God damn it, WHY?! Again…why the hell can't they just put a couple of extra D-class in there with instructions not to touch the painting?

"SCP-1759 has displayed hostile intent at my presence. Unknown if its influence forced D-0542 to attempt shooting me, or if he came to that conclusion of his own accord."

Says that SCP-1759 hates him…then says it's unknown if SCP-1759 was behind the shooting. So…how does he know it hates him? Projecting much?

The concept, if delivered better, would put me at a +1…but I'm afraid the actual delivery is a -1 for me. So a neutral vote for the time being, and I'll be keeping an eye out to see what happens.

Please keep your critiques in collapsibles if they get even vaguely this length in the future. - Soulless

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If only every less-than-stellar newbie article could turn itself around like this. Good job, PowerTower.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now that my phone is working and let me log in, I can comment on this.

As a military aircraft fanatic, I decided to give this one a read and I must say, you did pretty well! Although it doesn't come off as particularily creepy (as, correct me if I'm wrong, 998 was and still is), I did find this to be very interesting regardless. With a bit of practice, you could make some great articles!

Take my upvote, good sir.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you can find a better picture of an A-20 Havoc from the right side, feel free to change it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That image looks terrible.
